title: GIL Escape & Native Extensions
subtitle: When one core isn't enough — escape the GIL with multiprocessing and drop to native code for CPU-bound work that actually parallelizes.
goal: Diagnose why CPython threads do not parallelize CPU-bound work, fan that work across processes with Pool / Process / ProcessPoolExecutor, move bulk data over zero-copy shared memory instead of pickling it, reason about IPC and fork cost so you only parallelize when speedup beats it, and mount native code via ctypes (libc and your own runtime-compiled .so) when the hot loop must leave Python entirely.

blocks.heading: One lock to rule them all: why threads don't parallelize CPU work
blocks.html:
CPython protects its object model with a single Global Interpreter Lock. Exactly one thread executes Python bytecode at a time; the interpreter hands the GIL around (roughly every 5 ms via sys.setswitchinterval) so threads interleave, but they never run Python code simultaneously. For I/O-bound work this is fine — a thread blocked in recv() drops the GIL, so other threads progress. For CPU-bound work it is fatal: four threads crunching numbers share one core's worth of bytecode throughput and add lock-handoff overhead on top, so threaded CPU code is often slower than single-threaded.

The escape hatch is processes. Each process has its own interpreter and its own GIL, so N processes genuinely run on N cores. That parallelism is not free: there is no shared address space, so arguments and results cross the process boundary by pickling, and spawning a process costs milliseconds. The entire skill of this tier is deciding when the parallel speedup outweighs that IPC + fork tax — and shrinking the tax with shared memory and native code.

(Free-threaded CPython 3.13+ can disable the GIL, but the portable, production-default answer in 2026 is still: threads for I/O, processes for CPU.)

blocks.caption: Threads vs processes on a CPU-bound loop — the GIL made visible
blocks.code:
import time, math
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor

def burn(n):                      # pure CPU: no I/O, never drops the GIL voluntarily
    s = 0.0
    for i in range(n):
        s += math.sin(i) * math.cos(i)
    return s

WORK = [3_000_000] * 4

if __name__ == "__main__":
    t = time.perf_counter()
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(4) as ex:
        list(ex.map(burn, WORK))
    print("threads:  %.2fs" % (time.perf_counter() - t))

    t = time.perf_counter()
    with ProcessPoolExecutor(4) as ex:
        list(ex.map(burn, WORK))
    print("processes:%.2fs" % (time.perf_counter() - t))
blocks.explain: Four CPU-bound tasks. With threads the GIL serializes the bytecode: wall time is ~4× one task plus handoff overhead. With processes each child gets its own GIL on its own core, so wall time collapses toward 1× (minus spawn/pickle cost). This single comparison is the entire justification for the multiprocessing module on CPU work.

blocks.html: Always guard the entry point. On the spawn start method (Windows, and macOS since 3.8) each child re-imports your module to rebuild the worker function. Without an if __name__ == "__main__": guard, the top-level code that creates the Pool runs again in every child — a fork bomb of recursively spawning processes. Module-level work that builds the pool must live under that guard.

blocks.heading: Pool, Process, and ProcessPoolExecutor — picking the right primitive
blocks.html:
Three APIs, one idea (fan work across interpreters), different ergonomics:

  • multiprocessing.Process — the raw primitive. You construct, start(), and join() each process yourself and move data over a Queue or Pipe. Maximum control, maximum boilerplate. Reach for it when each worker is long-lived and bespoke.
  • multiprocessing.Pool — a fixed set of reusable workers plus map / imap / apply_async. map preserves input order and blocks; imap_unordered streams results as they finish (better for skewed task durations). chunksize batches tasks to amortize per-task IPC.
  • concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor — the modern Executor interface: the same submit/map/as_completed API as the thread pool, so you can swap I/O→CPU strategies by changing one class name. Prefer it for new code.

All three pickle the callable and its arguments to the child and pickle the return value back. That means: top-level (picklable) functions only — no lambdas, no closures — and keep payloads small or use shared memory.

blocks.caption: Pool.map with chunksize, and imap_unordered for skewed work
blocks.code:
from multiprocessing import Pool

def work(x):
    return x * x

if __name__ == "__main__":
    with Pool(4) as p:
        # chunksize batches ~250 items per IPC round-trip instead of 1.
        squares = p.map(work, range(1000), chunksize=250)
        print(sum(squares))                      # 332833500

        # Results stream back in COMPLETION order — ideal when durations vary.
        for r in p.imap_unordered(work, range(5)):
            pass  # process each as it lands; no waiting on a slow straggler
blocks.explain: chunksize is the single biggest Pool tuning knob: with chunksize=1 a million tiny tasks pay a million pickle/IPC round-trips and the overhead dwarfs the work. Batch them. Use map for ordered results, imap_unordered when one slow task shouldn't block consuming the fast ones.

blocks.heading: The cost model: pickling, fork, and when NOT to parallelize
blocks.html:
Every task crossing the process boundary is serialized (pickle on the way in, pickle on the way out) and copied through a pipe. Spawning a worker costs single-digit milliseconds; sending a 50 MB argument costs the pickle + copy of 50 MB. The decision rule is brutally simple:

Parallelize only when per-task compute time ≫ per-task IPC time, and total compute / cores ≫ spawn cost.

A loop of a million trivial operations should never go to a process pool — the pickling alone will be 10× the work. A handful of heavy, long-running, CPU-bound chunks is the sweet spot. Two levers shrink the tax: (1) make tasks coarse (few big chunks, not many tiny ones — this is what chunksize does), and (2) don't move the data at all — put the big array in shared_memory and pass only its name, so workers read the same physical pages with zero copy.

blocks.caption: Zero-copy fan-out with multiprocessing.shared_memory
blocks.code:
from multiprocessing import shared_memory, Pool

def partial(args):
    name, lo, hi = args
    shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(name=name)        # attach existing block
    buf = memoryview(shm.buf).cast("q")                 # int64 view, no copy
    acc = sum(buf[i] for i in range(lo, hi))
    buf.release()                                       # MUST release before close
    shm.close()
    return acc

if __name__ == "__main__":
    n, w = 1000, 4
    shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(create=True, size=8 * n)
    mv = memoryview(shm.buf).cast("q")
    for i in range(n):
        mv[i] = i
    edges = [(i * n) // w for i in range(w + 1)]
    tasks = [(shm.name, edges[i], edges[i + 1]) for i in range(w)]
    try:
        with Pool(w) as p:
            print(sum(p.map(partial, tasks)))          # 499500
    finally:
        mv.release(); shm.close(); shm.unlink()        # creator must unlink
blocks.explain: Only the small (name, lo, hi) tuple is pickled to each worker; the 8*n-byte array is never copied — every process maps the same physical pages. Two lifecycle rules bite hard: release every exported memoryview before close() (else 'cannot close exported pointers'), and the CREATOR must unlink() exactly once or you leak a segment in /dev/shm.

blocks.heading: Dropping to native code with ctypes
blocks.html:
Sometimes even one core of Python bytecode is too slow and the answer isn't more processes — it's leaving the interpreter. ctypes loads any shared library and calls its C functions directly, no compiler-at-import and no build step for the standard library. You CDLL the library, then — critically — declare each function's argtypes and restype so ctypes marshals arguments and interprets the return value correctly. Skip restype and ctypes assumes int, silently truncating a 64-bit pointer or size_t on a 64-bit box — a classic, brutal bug.

Two tiers of native mounting: (1) call into a library that already exists — libc is always present, so strlen, memcmp, qsort are a function call away; (2) write your own hot loop in C, compile it to a .so (cc -O2 -shared -fPIC), and CDLL the result. A tight numeric kernel in C routinely beats the Python version by 50–200× because it skips per-iteration boxing, refcounting, and bytecode dispatch entirely.

blocks.caption: Calling libc through ctypes — declare the signature or get burned
blocks.code:
import ctypes, ctypes.util

libc = ctypes.CDLL(ctypes.util.find_library("c") or "libc.so.6")

# Without these declarations ctypes assumes int args/return and corrupts 64-bit values.
libc.strlen.restype = ctypes.c_size_t
libc.strlen.argtypes = [ctypes.c_char_p]
libc.strcmp.restype = ctypes.c_int
libc.strcmp.argtypes = [ctypes.c_char_p, ctypes.c_char_p]

print(libc.strlen(b"orderbook"))          # 9, computed in C
print(libc.strcmp(b"AAPL", b"MSFT") < 0)  # True (A sorts before M)
blocks.explain: find_library locates libc portably. argtypes/restype are not optional decoration: they tell ctypes how wide each value is and how to convert Python<->C. c_char_p marshals bytes to a const char*. Pass a str (not bytes) and you'll get a TypeError — C strings are bytes.

blocks.caption: Compile a C kernel at runtime and mount it with ctypes
blocks.code:
import ctypes, tempfile, subprocess, os

SRC = r'''
long long sumsq(long long n) {        /* sum of i*i for i in [0,n) */
    long long acc = 0;
    for (long long i = 0; i < n; i++) acc += i * i;
    return acc;
}'''

d = tempfile.mkdtemp()
c, so = os.path.join(d, "k.c"), os.path.join(d, "k.so")
open(c, "w").write(SRC)
subprocess.run([os.environ.get("CC", "cc"), "-O2", "-shared", "-fPIC", "-o", so, c], check=True)

lib = ctypes.CDLL(so)
lib.sumsq.restype = ctypes.c_longlong
lib.sumsq.argtypes = [ctypes.c_longlong]
print(lib.sumsq(1_000_000))            # 333332833333500000, ~100x faster than Python
blocks.explain: -shared -fPIC builds a position-independent shared object; -O2 lets the C compiler vectorize the loop. The C version avoids Python's per-iteration int boxing and bytecode dispatch entirely. This is exactly how a hot pricing/risk kernel gets dropped out of Python without rewriting the whole service.

blocks.question: You run a pure-Python CPU-bound function across 4 OS threads on a 4-core machine. Compared to running it once, wall-clock time is roughly:
options: ~the same or slightly worse — the GIL serializes bytecode and adds handoff overhead
blocks.explain: Only one thread holds the GIL and executes Python bytecode at a time, so CPU-bound threads can't run in parallel; you get ~1x throughput plus lock-handoff cost. To use all cores on CPU work you need processes (each with its own GIL).

blocks.question: Why pass shm.name (a string) to Pool workers instead of the big list itself?
options: Strings pickle faster than lists of the same length
Passing the list pickles and copies the entire array to every worker; the name lets each worker map the same physical pages with zero copy
Workers cannot receive list arguments at all
It avoids the GIL inside each worker
blocks.explain: Arguments cross the process boundary by pickling. A large list is serialized and copied into every child — O(workers x size) IPC. shared_memory keeps one physical copy; workers attach by name and read it directly, so only the tiny name + offsets are pickled.

blocks.question: You call libc.malloc via ctypes on a 64-bit Linux box but never set restype. What's the danger?
options: Nothing — ctypes infers the type from the call
ctypes defaults restype to int (32-bit), truncating the 64-bit pointer and corrupting it
malloc returns a Python int automatically
blocks.explain: ctypes assumes a C int return when restype is unset. On LP64, a pointer/size_t is 64 bits but int is 32 — the high half is dropped, giving a garbage/truncated pointer. Always declare restype (e.g. c_void_p) and argtypes for any non-trivial signature.

blocks.title: Parallelize a CPU-bound sum across processes
blocks.instructions: Use a multiprocessing.Pool to compute the sum of squares of 0..999999 across 4 workers. Split the range into 4 contiguous chunks, map a top-level worker, and print the total. Expected: 333332833333500000.
blocks.starter:
from multiprocessing import Pool

def chunk_sumsq(rng):
    lo, hi = rng
    # TODO: return sum(i*i for i in range(lo, hi))
    return 0

if __name__ == "__main__":
    n, w = 1_000_000, 4
    edges = [(i * n) // w for i in range(w + 1)]
    ranges = [(edges[i], edges[i + 1]) for i in range(w)]
    # TODO: with Pool(w) as p: parts = p.map(chunk_sumsq, ranges)
    parts = [0]
    print(sum(parts))
blocks.solution:
from multiprocessing import Pool

def chunk_sumsq(rng):
    lo, hi = rng
    return sum(i * i for i in range(lo, hi))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    n, w = 1_000_000, 4
    edges = [(i * n) // w for i in range(w + 1)]
    ranges = [(edges[i], edges[i + 1]) for i in range(w)]
    with Pool(w) as p:
        parts = p.map(chunk_sumsq, ranges)
    print(sum(parts))
blocks.passMsg: Four interpreters, four cores, one summed result. That's real parallelism — the worker is a top-level (picklable) function and the Pool fans it out.
blocks.failMsg: Fill in chunk_sumsq to return sum(i*i for i in range(lo, hi)) and use Pool(w).map over the ranges.

problems.title: Parallel prime sieve across a process pool
problems.instructions:
Count the primes below N by escaping the GIL: split [0, N) into exactly W contiguous balanced ranges and count each range in its own process via multiprocessing.Pool(W), then sum the partial counts. The result must be independent of W — that is the whole point of a correct parallel decomposition.

Use the cut points edges[i] = (i * N) // W for i in 0..W so the ranges are contiguous, non-overlapping, and cover [0, N) exactly. A number is prime by ordinary trial division up to isqrt(n); 0 and 1 are not prime.

Stdin: one line, two ints N W (with N ≥ 0, W ≥ 1).

Stdout: a single integer — the count of primes p with p < N. Print only the count (no timings).
problems.starter:
import sys, math
from multiprocessing import Pool

def count_primes(rng):
    """Count primes in the half-open range [lo, hi). Runs in a worker process."""
    lo, hi = rng
    if lo < 2:
        lo = 2
    c = 0
    for n in range(lo, hi):
        # TODO: trial-divide n by odd divisors up to isqrt(n); count if prime.
        pass
    return c

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    n = int(data[0]); w = int(data[1])
    # TODO: split [0, n) into exactly w contiguous ranges using the cut points
    #       edges[i] = (i * n) // w  for i in 0..w  (balanced, deterministic).
    # TODO: run count_primes on each range across a multiprocessing.Pool(w),
    #       then print the SUM of the partial counts (one integer, no timings).
    raise NotImplementedError

main()
problems.solution:
import sys, math
from multiprocessing import Pool

def count_primes(rng):
    lo, hi = rng
    if lo < 2:
        lo = 2
    c = 0
    for n in range(lo, hi):
        if n == 2:
            c += 1
            continue
        if n % 2 == 0:
            continue
        r = int(math.isqrt(n))
        is_p = True
        for d in range(3, r + 1, 2):
            if n % d == 0:
                is_p = False
                break
        if is_p:
            c += 1
    return c

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    n = int(data[0]); w = int(data[1])
    edges = [(i * n) // w for i in range(w + 1)]
    ranges = [(edges[i], edges[i + 1]) for i in range(w)]
    with Pool(processes=w) as pool:
        parts = pool.map(count_primes, ranges)
    print(sum(parts))

main()
problems.hint: Make count_primes(lo, hi) a top-level function (picklable). Build ranges from edges[i]=(i*N)//W, Pool(W).map over them, and print the sum. Trial-divide only by odd numbers up to isqrt(n).

problems.title: Deterministic map-reduce over a ProcessPoolExecutor
problems.instructions:
Fan a deterministic workload across a concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=W) and reduce the per-chunk results into global statistics. There are CHUNKS chunks; chunk i is seeded with (i+1). Each chunk generates COUNT values from a linear-congruential generator and folds them into three statistics.

For each chunk, starting from x = seed, repeat COUNT times: x = (1103515245 * x + 12345) & 0x7fffffff, then v = x % MOD. Track per-chunk sum(v), max(v), and the count of v with v % 7 == 0. Reduce across chunks into a global sum, a global max, and a global div-by-7 count. The output must be identical regardless of W (correct map-reduce is associative).

Stdin: one line, four ints W CHUNKS COUNT MOD.

Stdout: one line, three ints space-separated: <global_sum> <global_max> <global_div7>.
problems.starter:
import sys
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

def chunk_stats(args):
    """Generate `count` LCG values in [0, mod); return (sum, max, count_div_by_7)."""
    seed, count, mod = args
    x = seed
    s = 0; mx = -1; d7 = 0
    for _ in range(count):
        # TODO: advance the LCG: x = (1103515245 * x + 12345) & 0x7fffffff
        # TODO: v = x % mod ; fold v into s, mx, and d7 (v % 7 == 0).
        pass
    return (s, mx, d7)

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    w = int(data[0]); chunks = int(data[1]); count = int(data[2]); mod = int(data[3])
    # TODO: build `chunks` tasks, chunk i seeded with (i + 1).
    # TODO: map them over a ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=w).
    # TODO: reduce across chunks -> global sum, global max, global div-by-7 count.
    # Print: "<sum> <max> <div7>"  (worker count must NOT change the answer).
    raise NotImplementedError

main()
problems.solution:
import sys
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

def chunk_stats(args):
    seed, count, mod = args
    x = seed
    s = 0; mx = -1; d7 = 0
    for _ in range(count):
        x = (1103515245 * x + 12345) & 0x7fffffff
        v = x % mod
        s += v
        if v > mx:
            mx = v
        if v % 7 == 0:
            d7 += 1
    return (s, mx, d7)

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    w = int(data[0]); chunks = int(data[1]); count = int(data[2]); mod = int(data[3])
    tasks = [(1 + i, count, mod) for i in range(chunks)]
    with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=w) as ex:
        results = list(ex.map(chunk_stats, tasks))
    total = sum(r[0] for r in results)
    gmax = max(r[1] for r in results)
    gd7 = sum(r[2] for r in results)
    print(f"{total} {gmax} {gd7}")

main()
problems.hint: Make chunk_stats((seed,count,mod)) a top-level function returning (sum,max,div7). Build tasks with seed=i+1, ex.map over them, then reduce: sum the sums, max the maxes, sum the div7 counts. Worker count must not change the math.

problems.title: Mount libc through ctypes: strlen + strcmp
problems.instructions:
Leave the interpreter for the hot operations: load libc via ctypes and use its strlen and strcmp — not Python's len or comparison operators — to analyze a list of tokens.

Load libc with ctypes.CDLL(ctypes.util.find_library("c") or "libc.so.6"). Declare strlen.restype = c_size_t, strlen.argtypes = [c_char_p], strcmp.restype = c_int, strcmp.argtypes = [c_char_p, c_char_p]. Encode each token to bytes. Compute total = sum of strlen over all tokens, and asc = the number of adjacent pairs (token[i], token[i+1]) for which strcmp(token[i], token[i+1]) < 0 (strictly ascending in C byte order).

Stdin: line 1 = integer n; then n whitespace-separated tokens (one per line). Tokens contain no spaces and are ASCII.

Stdout: one line, two ints space-separated: <total> <asc>.
problems.starter:
import sys, ctypes, ctypes.util

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    n = int(data[0])
    strs = data[1:1 + n]

    # TODO: load libc via ctypes (find_library("c") or "libc.so.6").
    # TODO: declare strlen -> size_t(c_char_p) and strcmp -> int(c_char_p, c_char_p).
    # TODO: encode each token to bytes; total = sum of libc.strlen over all tokens.
    # TODO: asc = number of ADJACENT pairs (i, i+1) with libc.strcmp(a, b) < 0.
    # Print: "<total> <asc>"
    raise NotImplementedError

main()
problems.solution:
import sys, ctypes, ctypes.util

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    n = int(data[0])
    strs = data[1:1 + n]

    libc_name = ctypes.util.find_library("c") or "libc.so.6"
    libc = ctypes.CDLL(libc_name)
    libc.strlen.restype = ctypes.c_size_t
    libc.strlen.argtypes = [ctypes.c_char_p]
    libc.strcmp.restype = ctypes.c_int
    libc.strcmp.argtypes = [ctypes.c_char_p, ctypes.c_char_p]

    bs = [s.encode() for s in strs]
    total = sum(libc.strlen(b) for b in bs)
    asc = 0
    for i in range(len(bs) - 1):
        if libc.strcmp(bs[i], bs[i + 1]) < 0:
            asc += 1
    print(f"{total} {asc}")

main()
problems.hint: Set argtypes/restype before calling or ctypes assumes int and truncates size_t. Pass bytes (token.encode()), not str, to c_char_p. asc counts only adjacent pairs where libc.strcmp(a,b) < 0.

problems.title: Zero-copy parallel reduction over shared memory
problems.instructions:
Compute the sum of squares of an integer array in parallel without pickling the array to every worker. Put the data in a multiprocessing.shared_memory.SharedMemory block once; each of W workers attaches by name and reduces a contiguous slice, so only the block name + offsets cross the process boundary.

Create a block of 8 * N bytes, write the values as int64 (memoryview(shm.buf).cast("q")). Split [0, N) into W ranges via edges[i] = (i * N) // W. Each worker attaches, sums v*v over its slice, releases its memoryview before close(), and returns its partial. Sum the partials. In a finally, release the writer view, close(), and unlink() the block exactly once (the creator owns cleanup).

Stdin: line 1 = two ints N W; line 2 = N space-separated ints (may be negative; fit in int64).

Stdout: a single integer — the sum of squares. Independent of W.
problems.starter:
import sys
from multiprocessing import shared_memory, Pool

def worker(args):
    """Sum of squares over buf[lo:hi], reading from the shared block by name."""
    shm_name, lo, hi = args
    shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(name=shm_name)
    buf = memoryview(shm.buf).cast("q")  # int64 view onto the shared block
    acc = 0
    # TODO: acc += buf[i] * buf[i] for i in [lo, hi)
    buf.release()   # release the exported pointer BEFORE closing (or close() errors)
    shm.close()
    return acc

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    n = int(data[0]); w = int(data[1])
    vals = [int(x) for x in data[2:2 + n]]

    # TODO: create a SharedMemory block of 8*n bytes, write vals into an int64 view.
    # TODO: split [0, n) into w ranges (edges[i] = i*n//w) and Pool.map `worker`.
    # TODO: print the summed sum-of-squares; in `finally`, release the view,
    #       close, and UNLINK the block (no leaked /dev/shm segments).
    raise NotImplementedError

main()
problems.solution:
import sys
from multiprocessing import shared_memory, Pool

def worker(args):
    shm_name, lo, hi = args
    shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(name=shm_name)
    buf = memoryview(shm.buf).cast("q")
    acc = 0
    for i in range(lo, hi):
        v = buf[i]
        acc += v * v
    buf.release()
    shm.close()
    return acc

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    n = int(data[0]); w = int(data[1])
    vals = [int(x) for x in data[2:2 + n]]

    shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(create=True, size=max(8, 8 * n))
    mv = memoryview(shm.buf).cast("q")
    for i in range(n):
        mv[i] = vals[i]
    edges = [(i * n) // w for i in range(w + 1)]
    tasks = [(shm.name, edges[i], edges[i + 1]) for i in range(w)]
    try:
        with Pool(processes=w) as pool:
            parts = pool.map(worker, tasks)
        print(sum(parts))
    finally:
        mv.release()
        shm.close()
        shm.unlink()

main()

tests.name: negatives and W not dividing N

problems.hint: Pass only (shm.name, lo, hi) to each worker — never the array. In the worker, buf = memoryview(shm.buf).cast('q'); call buf.release() before shm.close() or you get 'cannot close exported pointers'. The creator must unlink() once in a finally.

problems.title: Compile a native kernel at runtime and call it
problems.instructions:
When the hot loop must leave Python entirely, ship a C kernel: write it to a temp .c, compile a shared object with cc -O2 -shared -fPIC, and mount it with ctypes. The kernel counts points inside the Mandelbrot set on a fixed grid — a pure CPU-bound double-precision loop that is dramatically faster in C.

For a grid of WIDTH × HEIGHT pixels, pixel (px, py) maps to x0 = px/WIDTH*3.5 - 2.5, y0 = py/HEIGHT*2.0 - 1.0 (use double arithmetic, dividing by the integer dimension). Iterate x,y from 0 with x' = x*x - y*y + x0, y' = 2*x*y + y0 while x*x + y*y <= 4.0 and iter < MAX_ITER; a pixel is inside iff it reaches MAX_ITER. Count the inside pixels. You must do the inner loop in compiled C via ctypes, not in Python.

Stdin: one line, three ints WIDTH HEIGHT MAX_ITER.

Stdout: a single integer — the count of pixels inside the set.
problems.hint: Declare mandel_count.restype = c_longlong and argtypes = [c_longlong]*3, and use double (not float) in C so the arithmetic matches. Compile with -O2 -shared -fPIC -o out.so src.c (honor $CC), then ctypes.CDLL the .so.
